subject: ap-world-history
theme: Networks of Exchange, 1200–1450: Long-distance trade routes connect the Eastern Hemisphere, enabling the exchange of goods, ideas, diseases, and peoples across Afro-Eurasia.

core_concepts.title: The Silk Road: Land-Based Trade Networks
core_concepts.summary: The Silk Road was not a single road but a network of overland routes connecting China to the Mediterranean via Central Asia. Major commodities included silk, spices, glassware, cotton textiles, and precious metals. Trade was conducted by a chain of intermediaries — Central Asian merchants (Sogdians, later Turks) served as key middlemen. Beyond goods, the Silk Road transmitted religions (Buddhism, Islam, Christianity, Manichaeism), technologies, and diseases. The Mongol-created Pax Mongolica (c. 1250–1350) expanded Silk Road trade by enforcing security and reducing tolls.

core_concepts.title: The Indian Ocean Trade Network
core_concepts.summary: The Indian Ocean formed the world's largest and most dynamic pre-modern trading zone, connected by monsoon winds. Arab, Indian, Malay, and East African merchants sailed predictable seasonal routes: summer monsoons blew northeast to Africa; winter monsoons reversed. Major commodities included Indian cotton and spices, Chinese porcelain and silk, East African gold and ivory, and Arabian horses. The dhow — a lateen-sailed Arab vessel — was the workhorse of this trade. Major trading hubs included Calicut (India), Hormuz (Persian Gulf), Aden (Arabia), and Kilwa (East Africa).

core_concepts.title: The Trans-Saharan Trade Network
core_concepts.summary: Camel caravans began crossing the Sahara regularly after c. 300 CE, linking sub-Saharan West Africa with North Africa and the Mediterranean. Key commodities: West African gold and enslaved people flowed north; salt from Saharan mines (Taghaza) and North African manufactured goods flowed south. This trade sustained major West African empires (Ghana, Mali, Songhai) and enriched North African Muslim merchants. Islam spread into sub-Saharan Africa largely through this merchant network. The major Saharan entrepôt cities (Timbuktu, Djenné) grew wealthy serving this trade.

core_concepts.title: The Role of Middlemen and Diasporic Merchant Communities
core_concepts.summary: Long-distance trade depended on specialized merchant communities who established settlements along trade routes. Jewish merchants (Radhanites) connected Europe to the Islamic world. Arab merchants dominated the Indian Ocean from the Persian Gulf to East Africa. Chinese merchants (Huashang) established communities throughout Southeast Asia. These diasporic communities maintained trust networks, shared languages, and credit instruments (bills of exchange, suftaja) that enabled long-distance transactions without physical currency transfer. Their settlements often grew into cosmopolitan cities where multiple religions and cultures coexisted.

core_concepts.title: Technology and Innovation in Trade
core_concepts.summary: Several technological developments facilitated long-distance trade. The magnetic compass (Chinese invention, spread via Islamic merchants to Europe) enabled open-ocean navigation. Lateen sails allowed sailing against the wind, enabling more flexible voyaging. Camel saddle improvements made Saharan caravan trade economical. Paper money and letters of credit (developed in Tang-Song China and adopted in Islamic finance through hundi systems) reduced the dangers of carrying physical bullion. The development of commercial law (including the Islamic institution of qirad — merchant partnership with liability limits) provided institutional frameworks for investment.

core_concepts.title: Cultural and Religious Diffusion Through Trade
core_concepts.summary: Trade routes were highways for ideas as well as goods. Buddhism spread from India to Central and East Asia along the Silk Road. Islam spread along Indian Ocean trade routes into coastal East Africa, South Asia, and Southeast Asia — typically through peaceful merchant activity rather than conquest. Sufi mystics, who adapted Islam to local cultural practices, were especially effective missionaries in these contexts. The spread of Islam into West Africa came via trans-Saharan merchants. Technologies such as papermaking (China → Islamic world → Europe), numerals (India → Arabia → Europe), and agricultural crops diffused along these networks.

key_terms.definition: Network of overland trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean, facilitating exchange of goods (silk, spices), ideas, religions, and diseases across Eurasia.
Maritime trading system spanning from East Africa to East Asia, driven by predictable monsoon winds and connecting diverse merchant communities.
Seasonal wind patterns in the Indian Ocean — blowing northeast in summer, reversing in winter — that enabled predictable, regular maritime trade across the ocean.
Traditional Arab sailing vessel with a lateen (triangular) sail, the primary trading ship of the Indian Ocean from the Arabian Peninsula to East Africa and India.
Triangular sail that allows sailing at an angle to the wind, enabling more flexible navigation compared to square-rigged sails limited to sailing before the wind.
Relative peace and trade facilitation across the Mongol Empire (c. 1250–1350), enabling Marco Polo and Ibn Battuta-era cross-continental travel.
Roadside inn along Silk Road and Islamic trade routes that provided accommodation, water, and storage for traveling merchant caravans.
Settlement of merchants from a foreign land maintaining economic, cultural, and religious ties to their origin community while conducting trade in a host society.
Islamic financial instrument similar to a bill of exchange, allowing merchants to transfer value at a distance without physically transporting coins.
Islamic commercial partnership in which one party provides capital and another provides labor/expertise, with profits shared and losses limited to capital invested.
Venetian merchant (1254–1324) who traveled to China under the Pax Mongolica and whose account of Kublai Khan's court introduced Europeans to East Asian wealth.
Camel caravan trade routes crossing the Sahara, connecting West African gold and enslaved people to North African and Mediterranean markets.
Chinese navigational instrument that spread via Islamic and European traders, enabling open-ocean navigation by indicating magnetic north.
A port or trading center that acts as an intermediary hub where goods are imported, stored, and re-exported — connecting larger trade networks.
Bacterial disease (Yersinia pestis) spread by fleas on rodents, which traveled along Mongol-connected trade routes and caused the Black Death (1347–1352), killing 30–60% of Europe's population.

exam_tip: Trade network questions are among the most common on AP World History. Always identify: (1) what goods were traded, (2) what was exchanged beyond goods (ideas, religions, diseases), (3) which merchant groups dominated, and (4) what technologies enabled the trade. When comparing Silk Road vs. Indian Ocean networks, note that Indian Ocean trade was higher volume and more continuous than Silk Road overland trade. For DBQ and LEQ, practice stating 'Trade facilitated X by Y' — the causal mechanism matters more than the fact of trade itself.
